`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Note that in order to read the configuration file, Redis must be

`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Note on units: when memory size is needed, it is possible to specify

`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`################################## INCLUDES ###################################
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Include one or more other config files here.  This is useful if you
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# have a standard template that goes to all Redis servers but also need
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# to customize a few per-server settings.  Include files can include
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# other files, so use this wisely.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Note that option "include" won't be rewritten by command "CONFIG REWRITE"
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# from admin or Redis Sentinel. Since Redis always uses the last processed
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# line as value of a configuration directive, you'd better put includes
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# at the beginning of this file to avoid overwriting config change at runtime.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# If instead you are interested in using includes to override configuration
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# options, it is better to use include as the last line.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# include /path/to/local.conf
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# include /path/to/other.conf
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`################################## MODULES #####################################
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Load modules at startup. If the server is not able to load modules
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# it will abort. It is possible to use multiple loadmodule directives.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# loadmodule /path/to/my_module.so
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# loadmodule /path/to/other_module.so
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`################################## NETWORK #####################################
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# By default, if no "bind" configuration directive is specified, Redis listens
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# for connections from all available network interfaces on the host machine.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# It is possible to listen to just one or multiple selected interfaces using
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the "bind" configuration directive, followed by one or more IP addresses.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Examples:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# bind 192.168.1.100 10.0.0.1
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# bind 127.0.0.1 ::1
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# ~~~ WARNING ~~~ If the computer running Redis is directly exposed to the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# internet, binding to all the interfaces is dangerous and will expose the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# instance to everybody on the internet. So by default we uncomment the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# following bind directive, that will force Redis to listen only on the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# IPv4 loopback interface address (this means Redis will only be able to
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# accept client connections from the same host that it is running on).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# IF YOU ARE SURE YOU WANT YOUR INSTANCE TO LISTEN TO ALL THE INTERFACES
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# JUST COMMENT OUT THE FOLLOWING LINE.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# bind 127.0.0.1
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Protected mode is a layer of security protection, in order to avoid that
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Redis instances left open on the internet are accessed and exploited.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# When protected mode is on and if:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 1) The server is not binding explicitly to a set of addresses using the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    "bind" directive.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 2) No password is configured.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The server only accepts connections from clients connecting from the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# IPv4 and IPv6 loopback addresses 127.0.0.1 and ::1, and from Unix domain
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# sockets.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# By default protected mode is enabled. You should disable it only if
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# you are sure you want clients from other hosts to connect to Redis
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# even if no authentication is configured, nor a specific set of interfaces
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# are explicitly listed using the "bind" directive.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# protected-mode yes
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`protected-mode no
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Accept connections on the specified port, default is 6379 (IANA #815344).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# If port 0 is specified Redis will not listen on a TCP socket.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`port 6379
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# TCP listen() backlog.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# In high requests-per-second environments you need a high backlog in order
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# to avoid slow clients connection issues. Note that the Linux kernel
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# will silently truncate it to the value of /proc/sys/net/core/somaxconn so
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# make sure to raise both the value of somaxconn and tcp_max_syn_backlog
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# in order to get the desired effect.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`tcp-backlog 511
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Unix socket.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Specify the path for the Unix socket that will be used to listen for
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# incoming connections. There is no default, so Redis will not listen
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# on a unix socket when not specified.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# unixsocket /tmp/redis.sock
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# unixsocketperm 700
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Close the connection after a client is idle for N seconds (0 to disable)
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`timeout 0
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# TCP keepalive.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# If non-zero, use SO_KEEPALIVE to send TCP ACKs to clients in absence
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# of communication. This is useful for two reasons:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 1) Detect dead peers.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 2) Force network equipment in the middle to consider the connection to be
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    alive.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# On Linux, the specified value (in seconds) is the period used to send ACKs.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Note that to close the connection the double of the time is needed.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# On other kernels the period depends on the kernel configuration.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# A reasonable value for this option is 300 seconds, which is the new
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Redis default starting with Redis 3.2.1.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`tcp-keepalive 300
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`################################# TLS/SSL #####################################
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# By default, TLS/SSL is disabled. To enable it, the "tls-port" configuration
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# directive can be used to define TLS-listening ports. To enable TLS on the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# default port, use:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# port 0
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# tls-port 6379
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Configure a X.509 certificate and private key to use for authenticating the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# server to connected clients, masters or cluster peers.  These files should be
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# PEM formatted.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# tls-cert-file redis.crt 
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# tls-key-file redis.key
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Configure a DH parameters file to enable Diffie-Hellman (DH) key exchange:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# tls-dh-params-file redis.dh
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Configure a CA certificate(s) bundle or directory to authenticate TLS/SSL
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# clients and peers.  Redis requires an explicit configuration of at least one

`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# tls-ca-cert-file ca.crt

`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# By default, clients (including replica servers) on a TLS port are required
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# to authenticate using valid client side certificates.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# If "no" is specified, client certificates are not required and not accepted.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# If "optional" is specified, client certificates are accepted and must be
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# valid if provided, but are not required.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# tls-auth-clients no
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# tls-auth-clients optional
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# By default, a Redis replica does not attempt to establish a TLS connection
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# with its master.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Use the following directive to enable TLS on replication links.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# tls-replication yes
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# By default, the Redis Cluster bus uses a plain TCP connection. To enable
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# TLS for the bus protocol, use the following directive:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# tls-cluster yes
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Explicitly specify TLS versions to support. Allowed values are case insensitive
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# and include "TLSv1", "TLSv1.1", "TLSv1.2", "TLSv1.3" (OpenSSL >= 1.1.1) or
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# any combination. To enable only TLSv1.2 and TLSv1.3, use:

`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# tls-protocols "TLSv1.2 TLSv1.3"
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Configure allowed ciphers.  See the ciphers(1ssl) manpage for more information
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# about the syntax of this string.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Note: this configuration applies only to <= TLSv1.2.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# tls-ciphers DEFAULT:!MEDIUM
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Configure allowed TLSv1.3 ciphersuites.  See the ciphers(1ssl) manpage for more
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# information about the syntax of this string, and specifically for TLSv1.3
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# ciphersuites.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# tls-ciphersuites TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# When choosing a cipher, use the server's preference instead of the client
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# preference. By default, the server follows the client's preference.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# tls-prefer-server-ciphers yes
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# By default, TLS session caching is enabled to allow faster and less expensive
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# reconnections by clients that support it. Use the following directive to disable
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# caching.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# tls-session-caching no
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Change the default number of TLS sessions cached. A zero value sets the cache
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# to unlimited size. The default size is 20480.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# tls-session-cache-size 5000
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Change the default timeout of cached TLS sessions. The default timeout is 300
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# seconds.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# tls-session-cache-timeout 60
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`################################# GENERAL #####################################
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# By default Redis does not run as a daemon. Use 'yes' if you need it.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Note that Redis will write a pid file in /var/run/redis.pid when daemonized.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`daemonize no
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# If you run Redis from upstart or systemd, Redis can interact with your
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# supervision tree. Options:

`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   supervised upstart - signal upstart by putting Redis into SIGSTOP mode
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#                        requires "expect stop" in your upstart job config
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   supervised systemd - signal systemd by writing READY=1 to $NOTIFY_SOCKET
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   supervised auto    - detect upstart or systemd method based on
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#                        UPSTART_JOB or NOTIFY_SOCKET environment variables
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Note: these supervision methods only signal "process is ready."
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#       They do not enable continuous pings back to your supervisor.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`supervised no
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# If a pid file is specified, Redis writes it where specified at startup
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# and removes it at exit.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# When the server runs non daemonized, no pid file is created if none is
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# specified in the configuration. When the server is daemonized, the pid file
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# is used even if not specified, defaulting to "/var/run/redis.pid".
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Creating a pid file is best effort: if Redis is not able to create it
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# nothing bad happens, the server will start and run normally.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`pidfile /var/run/redis_6379.pid
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Specify the server verbosity level.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# This can be one of:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# debug (a lot of information, useful for development/testing)
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# verbose (many rarely useful info, but not a mess like the debug level)
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# notice (moderately verbose, what you want in production probably)
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# warning (only very important / critical messages are logged)
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`loglevel notice
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Specify the log file name. Also the empty string can be used to force
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Redis to log on the standard output. Note that if you use standard
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# output for logging but daemonize, logs will be sent to /dev/null
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`logfile ""
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# To enable logging to the system logger, just set 'syslog-enabled' to yes,
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# and optionally update the other syslog parameters to suit your needs.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# syslog-enabled no
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Specify the syslog identity.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# syslog-ident redis
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Specify the syslog facility. Must be USER or between LOCAL0-LOCAL7.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# syslog-facility local0
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Set the number of databases. The default database is DB 0, you can select
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# a different one on a per-connection basis using SELECT <dbid> where
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# dbid is a number between 0 and 'databases'-1
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`databases 16
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# By default Redis shows an ASCII art logo only when started to log to the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# standard output and if the standard output is a TTY. Basically this means
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# that normally a logo is displayed only in interactive sessions.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# However it is possible to force the pre-4.0 behavior and always show a
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# ASCII art logo in startup logs by setting the following option to yes.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`always-show-logo yes
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`################################ SNAPSHOTTING  ################################
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Save the DB on disk:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   save <seconds> <changes>
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   Will save the DB if both the given number of seconds and the given
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   number of write operations against the DB occurred.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   In the example below the behavior will be to save:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   after 900 sec (15 min) if at least 1 key changed
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   after 300 sec (5 min) if at least 10 keys changed
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   after 60 sec if at least 10000 keys changed
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   Note: you can disable saving completely by commenting out all "save" lines.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   It is also possible to remove all the previously configured save
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   points by adding a save directive with a single empty string argument
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   like in the following example:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   save ""
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`save 900 1
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`save 300 10
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`save 60 10000
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# By default Redis will stop accepting writes if RDB snapshots are enabled
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# (at least one save point) and the latest background save failed.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# This will make the user aware (in a hard way) that data is not persisting
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# on disk properly, otherwise chances are that no one will notice and some
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# disaster will happen.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# If the background saving process will start working again Redis will
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# automatically allow writes again.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# However if you have setup your proper monitoring of the Redis server
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# and persistence, you may want to disable this feature so that Redis will
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# continue to work as usual even if there are problems with disk,
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# permissions, and so forth.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`stop-writes-on-bgsave-error yes
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Compress string objects using LZF when dump .rdb databases?
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# By default compression is enabled as it's almost always a win.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# If you want to save some CPU in the saving child set it to 'no' but
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the dataset will likely be bigger if you have compressible values or keys.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`rdbcompression yes
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Since version 5 of RDB a CRC64 checksum is placed at the end of the file.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# This makes the format more resistant to corruption but there is a performance
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# hit to pay (around 10%) when saving and loading RDB files, so you can disable it
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# for maximum performances.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# RDB files created with checksum disabled have a checksum of zero that will
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# tell the loading code to skip the check.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`rdbchecksum yes
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The filename where to dump the DB
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`dbfilename dump.rdb
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Remove RDB files used by replication in instances without persistence
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# enabled. By default this option is disabled, however there are environments
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# where for regulations or other security concerns, RDB files persisted on
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# disk by masters in order to feed replicas, or stored on disk by replicas
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# in order to load them for the initial synchronization, should be deleted
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# ASAP. Note that this option ONLY WORKS in instances that have both AOF
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# and RDB persistence disabled, otherwise is completely ignored.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# An alternative (and sometimes better) way to obtain the same effect is
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# to use diskless replication on both master and replicas instances. However
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# in the case of replicas, diskless is not always an option.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`rdb-del-sync-files no
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The working directory.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The DB will be written inside this directory, with the filename specified
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# above using the 'dbfilename' configuration directive.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The Append Only File will also be created inside this directory.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Note that you must specify a directory here, not a file name.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`dir ./
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`################################# REPLICATION #################################
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Master-Replica replication. Use replicaof to make a Redis instance a copy of
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# another Redis server. A few things to understand ASAP about Redis replication.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   +------------------+      +---------------+
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   |      Master      | ---> |    Replica    |
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   | (receive writes) |      |  (exact copy) |
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   +------------------+      +---------------+
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 1) Redis replication is asynchronous, but you can configure a master to
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    stop accepting writes if it appears to be not connected with at least
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    a given number of replicas.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 2) Redis replicas are able to perform a partial resynchronization with the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    master if the replication link is lost for a relatively small amount of
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    time. You may want to configure the replication backlog size (see the next
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    sections of this file) with a sensible value depending on your needs.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 3) Replication is automatic and does not need user intervention. After a
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    network partition replicas automatically try to reconnect to masters
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    and resynchronize with them.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# replicaof <masterip> <masterport>
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# If the master is password protected (using the "requirepass" configuration
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# directive below) it is possible to tell the replica to authenticate before
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# starting the replication synchronization process, otherwise the master will
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# refuse the replica request.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# masterauth <master-password>
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# However this is not enough if you are using Redis ACLs (for Redis version
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 6 or greater), and the default user is not capable of running the PSYNC
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# command and/or other commands needed for replication. In this case it's
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# better to configure a special user to use with replication, and specify the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# masteruser configuration as such:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# masteruser <username>
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# When masteruser is specified, the replica will authenticate against its
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# master using the new AUTH form: AUTH <username> <password>.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# When a replica loses its connection with the master, or when the replication
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# is still in progress, the replica can act in two different ways:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 1) if replica-serve-stale-data is set to 'yes' (the default) the replica will
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    still reply to client requests, possibly with out of date data, or the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    data set may just be empty if this is the first synchronization.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 2) If replica-serve-stale-data is set to 'no' the replica will reply with
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    an error "SYNC with master in progress" to all commands except:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    INFO, REPLICAOF, AUTH, PING, SHUTDOWN, REPLCONF, ROLE, CONFIG, SUBSCRIBE,
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    UNSUBSCRIBE, PSUBSCRIBE, PUNSUBSCRIBE, PUBLISH, PUBSUB, COMMAND, POST,
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    HOST and LATENCY.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`replica-serve-stale-data yes
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# You can configure a replica instance to accept writes or not. Writing against
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# a replica instance may be useful to store some ephemeral data (because data
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# written on a replica will be easily deleted after resync with the master) but
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# may also cause problems if clients are writing to it because of a
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# misconfiguration.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Since Redis 2.6 by default replicas are read-only.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Note: read only replicas are not designed to be exposed to untrusted clients
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# on the internet. It's just a protection layer against misuse of the instance.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Still a read only replica exports by default all the administrative commands
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# such as CONFIG, DEBUG, and so forth. To a limited extent you can improve
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# security of read only replicas using 'rename-command' to shadow all the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# administrative / dangerous commands.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`replica-read-only yes
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Replication SYNC strategy: disk or socket.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# New replicas and reconnecting replicas that are not able to continue the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# replication process just receiving differences, need to do what is called a
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# "full synchronization". An RDB file is transmitted from the master to the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# replicas.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The transmission can happen in two different ways:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 1) Disk-backed: The Redis master creates a new process that writes the RDB
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#                 file on disk. Later the file is transferred by the parent
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#                 process to the replicas incrementally.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 2) Diskless: The Redis master creates a new process that directly writes the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#              RDB file to replica sockets, without touching the disk at all.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# With disk-backed replication, while the RDB file is generated, more replicas
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# can be queued and served with the RDB file as soon as the current child
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# producing the RDB file finishes its work. With diskless replication instead
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# once the transfer starts, new replicas arriving will be queued and a new
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# transfer will start when the current one terminates.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# When diskless replication is used, the master waits a configurable amount of
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# time (in seconds) before starting the transfer in the hope that multiple
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# replicas will arrive and the transfer can be parallelized.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# With slow disks and fast (large bandwidth) networks, diskless replication
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# works better.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`repl-diskless-sync no
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# When diskless replication is enabled, it is possible to configure the delay
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the server waits in order to spawn the child that transfers the RDB via socket
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# to the replicas.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# This is important since once the transfer starts, it is not possible to serve
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# new replicas arriving, that will be queued for the next RDB transfer, so the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# server waits a delay in order to let more replicas arrive.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The delay is specified in seconds, and by default is 5 seconds. To disable
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# it entirely just set it to 0 seconds and the transfer will start ASAP.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`repl-diskless-sync-delay 5
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# WARNING: RDB diskless load is experimental. Since in this setup the replica
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# does not immediately store an RDB on disk, it may cause data loss during
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# failovers. RDB diskless load + Redis modules not handling I/O reads may also
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# cause Redis to abort in case of I/O errors during the initial synchronization
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# stage with the master. Use only if your do what you are doing.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# -----------------------------------------------------------------------------
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Replica can load the RDB it reads from the replication link directly from the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# socket, or store the RDB to a file and read that file after it was completely
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# received from the master.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# In many cases the disk is slower than the network, and storing and loading
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the RDB file may increase replication time (and even increase the master's
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Copy on Write memory and salve buffers).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# However, parsing the RDB file directly from the socket may mean that we have
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# to flush the contents of the current database before the full rdb was
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# received. For this reason we have the following options:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# "disabled"    - Don't use diskless load (store the rdb file to the disk first)
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# "on-empty-db" - Use diskless load only when it is completely safe.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# "swapdb"      - Keep a copy of the current db contents in RAM while parsing
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#                 the data directly from the socket. note that this requires
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#                 sufficient memory, if you don't have it, you risk an OOM kill.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`repl-diskless-load disabled
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Replicas send PINGs to server in a predefined interval. It's possible to
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# change this interval with the repl_ping_replica_period option. The default
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# value is 10 seconds.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# repl-ping-replica-period 10
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The following option sets the replication timeout for:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 1) Bulk transfer I/O during SYNC, from the point of view of replica.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 2) Master timeout from the point of view of replicas (data, pings).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 3) Replica timeout from the point of view of masters (REPLCONF ACK pings).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# It is important to make sure that this value is greater than the value
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# specified for repl-ping-replica-period otherwise a timeout will be detected
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# every time there is low traffic between the master and the replica. The default
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# value is 60 seconds.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# repl-timeout 60
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Disable TCP_NODELAY on the replica socket after SYNC?
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# If you select "yes" Redis will use a smaller number of TCP packets and
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# less bandwidth to send data to replicas. But this can add a delay for
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the data to appear on the replica side, up to 40 milliseconds with
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Linux kernels using a default configuration.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# If you select "no" the delay for data to appear on the replica side will
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# be reduced but more bandwidth will be used for replication.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# By default we optimize for low latency, but in very high traffic conditions
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# or when the master and replicas are many hops away, turning this to "yes" may
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# be a good idea.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`repl-disable-tcp-nodelay no
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Set the replication backlog size. The backlog is a buffer that accumulates
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# replica data when replicas are disconnected for some time, so that when a
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# replica wants to reconnect again, often a full resync is not needed, but a
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# partial resync is enough, just passing the portion of data the replica
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# missed while disconnected.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The bigger the replication backlog, the longer the replica can endure the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# disconnect and later be able to perform a partial resynchronization.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The backlog is only allocated if there is at least one replica connected.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# repl-backlog-size 1mb
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# After a master has no connected replicas for some time, the backlog will be
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# freed. The following option configures the amount of seconds that need to
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# elapse, starting from the time the last replica disconnected, for the backlog
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# buffer to be freed.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Note that replicas never free the backlog for timeout, since they may be
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# promoted to masters later, and should be able to correctly "partially
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# resynchronize" with other replicas: hence they should always accumulate backlog.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# A value of 0 means to never release the backlog.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# repl-backlog-ttl 3600
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The replica priority is an integer number published by Redis in the INFO
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# output. It is used by Redis Sentinel in order to select a replica to promote
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# into a master if the master is no longer working correctly.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# A replica with a low priority number is considered better for promotion, so
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# for instance if there are three replicas with priority 10, 100, 25 Sentinel
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# will pick the one with priority 10, that is the lowest.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# However a special priority of 0 marks the replica as not able to perform the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# role of master, so a replica with priority of 0 will never be selected by
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Redis Sentinel for promotion.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# By default the priority is 100.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`replica-priority 100
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# It is possible for a master to stop accepting writes if there are less than
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# N replicas connected, having a lag less or equal than M seconds.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The N replicas need to be in "online" state.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The lag in seconds, that must be <= the specified value, is calculated from
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the last ping received from the replica, that is usually sent every second.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# This option does not GUARANTEE that N replicas will accept the write, but
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# will limit the window of exposure for lost writes in case not enough replicas
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# are available, to the specified number of seconds.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# For example to require at least 3 replicas with a lag <= 10 seconds use:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# min-replicas-to-write 3
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# min-replicas-max-lag 10
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Setting one or the other to 0 disables the feature.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# By default min-replicas-to-write is set to 0 (feature disabled) and
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# min-replicas-max-lag is set to 10.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# A Redis master is able to list the address and port of the attached
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# replicas in different ways. For example the "INFO replication" section
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# offers this information, which is used, among other tools, by
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Redis Sentinel in order to discover replica instances.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Another place where this info is available is in the output of the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# "ROLE" command of a master.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The listed IP address and port normally reported by a replica is
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# obtained in the following way:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   IP: The address is auto detected by checking the peer address
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   of the socket used by the replica to connect with the master.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   Port: The port is communicated by the replica during the replication
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   handshake, and is normally the port that the replica is using to
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   listen for connections.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# However when port forwarding or Network Address Translation (NAT) is
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# used, the replica may actually be reachable via different IP and port
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# pairs. The following two options can be used by a replica in order to
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# report to its master a specific set of IP and port, so that both INFO
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# and ROLE will report those values.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# There is no need to use both the options if you need to override just
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the port or the IP address.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# replica-announce-ip 5.5.5.5
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# replica-announce-port 1234
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`############################### KEYS TRACKING #################################
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Redis implements server assisted support for client side caching of values.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# This is implemented using an invalidation table that remembers, using
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 16 millions of slots, what clients may have certain subsets of keys. In turn
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# this is used in order to send invalidation messages to clients. Please
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# check this page to understand more about the feature:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   https://redis.io/topics/client-side-caching
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# When tracking is enabled for a client, all the read only queries are assumed
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# to be cached: this will force Redis to store information in the invalidation
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# table. When keys are modified, such information is flushed away, and
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# invalidation messages are sent to the clients. However if the workload is
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# heavily dominated by reads, Redis could use more and more memory in order
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# to track the keys fetched by many clients.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# For this reason it is possible to configure a maximum fill value for the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# invalidation table. By default it is set to 1M of keys, and once this limit
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# is reached, Redis will start to evict keys in the invalidation table
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# even if they were not modified, just to reclaim memory: this will in turn
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# force the clients to invalidate the cached values. Basically the table
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# maximum size is a trade off between the memory you want to spend server
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# side to track information about who cached what, and the ability of clients
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# to retain cached objects in memory.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# If you set the value to 0, it means there are no limits, and Redis will
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# retain as many keys as needed in the invalidation table.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# In the "stats" INFO section, you can find information about the number of
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# keys in the invalidation table at every given moment.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Note: when key tracking is used in broadcasting mode, no memory is used
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# in the server side so this setting is useless.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# tracking-table-max-keys 1000000
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`################################## SECURITY ###################################
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Warning: since Redis is pretty fast, an outside user can try up to
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 1 million passwords per second against a modern box. This means that you
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# should use very strong passwords, otherwise they will be very easy to break.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Note that because the password is really a shared secret between the client
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# and the server, and should not be memorized by any human, the password
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# can be easily a long string from /dev/urandom or whatever, so by using a
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# long and unguessable password no brute force attack will be possible.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Redis ACL users are defined in the following format:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   user <username> ... acl rules ...
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# For example:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   user worker +@list +@connection ~jobs:* on >ffa9203c493aa99
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The special username "default" is used for new connections. If this user
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# has the "nopass" rule, then new connections will be immediately authenticated
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# as the "default" user without the need of any password provided via the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# AUTH command. Otherwise if the "default" user is not flagged with "nopass"
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the connections will start in not authenticated state, and will require
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# AUTH (or the HELLO command AUTH option) in order to be authenticated and
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# start to work.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The ACL rules that describe what a user can do are the following:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  on           Enable the user: it is possible to authenticate as this user.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  off          Disable the user: it's no longer possible to authenticate
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#               with this user, however the already authenticated connections
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#               will still work.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  +<command>   Allow the execution of that command
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  -<command>   Disallow the execution of that command
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  +@<category> Allow the execution of all the commands in such category
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#               with valid categories are like @admin, @set, @sortedset, ...
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#               and so forth, see the full list in the server.c file where
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#               the Redis command table is described and defined.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#               The special category @all means all the commands, but currently
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#               present in the server, and that will be loaded in the future
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#               via modules.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  +<command>|subcommand    Allow a specific subcommand of an otherwise
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#                           disabled command. Note that this form is not
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#                           allowed as negative like -DEBUG|SEGFAULT, but
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#                           only additive starting with "+".
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  allcommands  Alias for +@all. Note that it implies the ability to execute
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#               all the future commands loaded via the modules system.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  nocommands   Alias for -@all.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  ~<pattern>   Add a pattern of keys that can be mentioned as part of
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#               commands. For instance ~* allows all the keys. The pattern
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#               is a glob-style pattern like the one of KEYS.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#               It is possible to specify multiple patterns.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  allkeys      Alias for ~*
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  resetkeys    Flush the list of allowed keys patterns.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  ><password>  Add this password to the list of valid password for the user.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#               For example >mypass will add "mypass" to the list.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#               This directive clears the "nopass" flag (see later).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  <<password>  Remove this password from the list of valid passwords.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  nopass       All the set passwords of the user are removed, and the user
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#               is flagged as requiring no password: it means that every
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#               password will work against this user. If this directive is
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#               used for the default user, every new connection will be
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#               immediately authenticated with the default user without
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#               any explicit AUTH command required. Note that the "resetpass"
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#               directive will clear this condition.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  resetpass    Flush the list of allowed passwords. Moreover removes the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#               "nopass" status. After "resetpass" the user has no associated
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#               passwords and there is no way to authenticate without adding
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#               some password (or setting it as "nopass" later).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  reset        Performs the following actions: resetpass, resetkeys, off,
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#               -@all. The user returns to the same state it has immediately
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#               after its creation.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# ACL rules can be specified in any order: for instance you can start with
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# passwords, then flags, or key patterns. However note that the additive
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# and subtractive rules will CHANGE MEANING depending on the ordering.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# For instance see the following example:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   user alice on +@all -DEBUG ~* >somepassword
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# This will allow "alice" to use all the commands with the exception of the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# DEBUG command, since +@all added all the commands to the set of the commands
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# alice can use, and later DEBUG was removed. However if we invert the order
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# of two ACL rules the result will be different:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   user alice on -DEBUG +@all ~* >somepassword
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Now DEBUG was removed when alice had yet no commands in the set of allowed
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# commands, later all the commands are added, so the user will be able to
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# execute everything.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Basically ACL rules are processed left-to-right.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# For more information about ACL configuration please refer to
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the Redis web site at https://redis.io/topics/acl
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# ACL LOG
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The ACL Log tracks failed commands and authentication events associated
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# with ACLs. The ACL Log is useful to troubleshoot failed commands blocked 
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# by ACLs. The ACL Log is stored in memory. You can reclaim memory with 
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# ACL LOG RESET. Define the maximum entry length of the ACL Log below.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`acllog-max-len 128
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Using an external ACL file
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Instead of configuring users here in this file, it is possible to use
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# a stand-alone file just listing users. The two methods cannot be mixed:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# if you configure users here and at the same time you activate the external
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# ACL file, the server will refuse to start.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The format of the external ACL user file is exactly the same as the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# format that is used inside redis.conf to describe users.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# aclfile /etc/redis/users.acl
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# IMPORTANT NOTE: starting with Redis 6 "requirepass" is just a compatibility
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# layer on top of the new ACL system. The option effect will be just setting
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the password for the default user. Clients will still authenticate using
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# AUTH <password> as usually, or more explicitly with AUTH default <password>
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# if they follow the new protocol: both will work.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`requirepass 123456
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Command renaming (DEPRECATED).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# ------------------------------------------------------------------------
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# WARNING: avoid using this option if possible. Instead use ACLs to remove
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# commands from the default user, and put them only in some admin user you
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# create for administrative purposes.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# ------------------------------------------------------------------------
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# It is possible to change the name of dangerous commands in a shared
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# environment. For instance the CONFIG command may be renamed into something
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# hard to guess so that it will still be available for internal-use tools
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# but not available for general clients.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Example:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# rename-command CONFIG b840fc02d524045429941cc15f59e41cb7be6c52
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# It is also possible to completely kill a command by renaming it into
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# an empty string:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# rename-command CONFIG ""
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Please note that changing the name of commands that are logged into the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# AOF file or transmitted to replicas may cause problems.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`################################### CLIENTS ####################################
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Set the max number of connected clients at the same time. By default
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# this limit is set to 10000 clients, however if the Redis server is not
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# able to configure the process file limit to allow for the specified limit
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the max number of allowed clients is set to the current file limit
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# minus 32 (as Redis reserves a few file descriptors for internal uses).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Once the limit is reached Redis will close all the new connections sending
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# an error 'max number of clients reached'.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# IMPORTANT: When Redis Cluster is used, the max number of connections is also
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# shared with the cluster bus: every node in the cluster will use two
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# connections, one incoming and another outgoing. It is important to size the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# limit accordingly in case of very large clusters.
