`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# maxclients 10000
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`############################## MEMORY MANAGEMENT ################################
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Set a memory usage limit to the specified amount of bytes.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# When the memory limit is reached Redis will try to remove keys
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# according to the eviction policy selected (see maxmemory-policy).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# If Redis can't remove keys according to the policy, or if the policy is
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# set to 'noeviction', Redis will start to reply with errors to commands
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# that would use more memory, like SET, LPUSH, and so on, and will continue
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# to reply to read-only commands like GET.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# This option is usually useful when using Redis as an LRU or LFU cache, or to
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# set a hard memory limit for an instance (using the 'noeviction' policy).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# WARNING: If you have replicas attached to an instance with maxmemory on,
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the size of the output buffers needed to feed the replicas are subtracted
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# from the used memory count, so that network problems / resyncs will
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# not trigger a loop where keys are evicted, and in turn the output
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# buffer of replicas is full with DELs of keys evicted triggering the deletion
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# of more keys, and so forth until the database is completely emptied.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# In short... if you have replicas attached it is suggested that you set a lower
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# limit for maxmemory so that there is some free RAM on the system for replica
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# output buffers (but this is not needed if the policy is 'noeviction').
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# maxmemory <bytes>
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# MAXMEMORY POLICY: how Redis will select what to remove when maxmemory
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# is reached. You can select one from the following behaviors:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# volatile-lru -> Evict using approximated LRU, only keys with an expire set.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# allkeys-lru -> Evict any key using approximated LRU.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# volatile-lfu -> Evict using approximated LFU, only keys with an expire set.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# allkeys-lfu -> Evict any key using approximated LFU.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# volatile-random -> Remove a random key having an expire set.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# allkeys-random -> Remove a random key, any key.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# volatile-ttl -> Remove the key with the nearest expire time (minor TTL)
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# noeviction -> Don't evict anything, just return an error on write operations.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# LRU means Least Recently Used
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# LFU means Least Frequently Used
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Both LRU, LFU and volatile-ttl are implemented using approximated
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# randomized algorithms.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Note: with any of the above policies, Redis will return an error on write
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#       operations, when there are no suitable keys for eviction.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#       At the date of writing these commands are: set setnx setex append
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#       incr decr rpush lpush rpushx lpushx linsert lset rpoplpush sadd
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#       sinter sinterstore sunion sunionstore sdiff sdiffstore zadd zincrby
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#       zunionstore zinterstore hset hsetnx hmset hincrby incrby decrby
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#       getset mset msetnx exec sort
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The default is:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# maxmemory-policy noeviction
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# LRU, LFU and minimal TTL algorithms are not precise algorithms but approximated
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# algorithms (in order to save memory), so you can tune it for speed or
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# accuracy. By default Redis will check five keys and pick the one that was
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# used least recently, you can change the sample size using the following
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# configuration directive.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The default of 5 produces good enough results. 10 Approximates very closely
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# true LRU but costs more CPU. 3 is faster but not very accurate.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# maxmemory-samples 5
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Starting from Redis 5, by default a replica will ignore its maxmemory setting
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# (unless it is promoted to master after a failover or manually). It means
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# that the eviction of keys will be just handled by the master, sending the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# DEL commands to the replica as keys evict in the master side.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# This behavior ensures that masters and replicas stay consistent, and is usually
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# what you want, however if your replica is writable, or you want the replica
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# to have a different memory setting, and you are sure all the writes performed
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# to the replica are idempotent, then you may change this default (but be sure
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# to understand what you are doing).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Note that since the replica by default does not evict, it may end using more
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# memory than the one set via maxmemory (there are certain buffers that may
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# be larger on the replica, or data structures may sometimes take more memory
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# and so forth). So make sure you monitor your replicas and make sure they
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# have enough memory to never hit a real out-of-memory condition before the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# master hits the configured maxmemory setting.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# replica-ignore-maxmemory yes
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Redis reclaims expired keys in two ways: upon access when those keys are
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# found to be expired, and also in background, in what is called the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# "active expire key". The key space is slowly and interactively scanned
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# looking for expired keys to reclaim, so that it is possible to free memory
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# of keys that are expired and will never be accessed again in a short time.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The default effort of the expire cycle will try to avoid having more than
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# ten percent of expired keys still in memory, and will try to avoid consuming
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# more than 25% of total memory and to add latency to the system. However
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# it is possible to increase the expire "effort" that is normally set to
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# "1", to a greater value, up to the value "10". At its maximum value the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# system will use more CPU, longer cycles (and technically may introduce
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# more latency), and will tolerate less already expired keys still present
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# in the system. It's a tradeoff between memory, CPU and latency.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# active-expire-effort 1
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`############################# LAZY FREEING ####################################
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Redis has two primitives to delete keys. One is called DEL and is a blocking
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# deletion of the object. It means that the server stops processing new commands
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# in order to reclaim all the memory associated with an object in a synchronous
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# way. If the key deleted is associated with a small object, the time needed
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# in order to execute the DEL command is very small and comparable to most other
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# O(1) or O(log_N) commands in Redis. However if the key is associated with an
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# aggregated value containing millions of elements, the server can block for
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# a long time (even seconds) in order to complete the operation.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# For the above reasons Redis also offers non blocking deletion primitives
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# such as UNLINK (non blocking DEL) and the ASYNC option of FLUSHALL and
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# FLUSHDB commands, in order to reclaim memory in background. Those commands
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# are executed in constant time. Another thread will incrementally free the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# object in the background as fast as possible.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# DEL, UNLINK and ASYNC option of FLUSHALL and FLUSHDB are user-controlled.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# It's up to the design of the application to understand when it is a good
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# idea to use one or the other. However the Redis server sometimes has to
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# delete keys or flush the whole database as a side effect of other operations.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Specifically Redis deletes objects independently of a user call in the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# following scenarios:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 1) On eviction, because of the maxmemory and maxmemory policy configurations,
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    in order to make room for new data, without going over the specified
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    memory limit.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 2) Because of expire: when a key with an associated time to live (see the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    EXPIRE command) must be deleted from memory.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 3) Because of a side effect of a command that stores data on a key that may
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    already exist. For example the RENAME command may delete the old key
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    content when it is replaced with another one. Similarly SUNIONSTORE
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    or SORT with STORE option may delete existing keys. The SET command
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    itself removes any old content of the specified key in order to replace
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    it with the specified string.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 4) During replication, when a replica performs a full resynchronization with
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    its master, the content of the whole database is removed in order to
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    load the RDB file just transferred.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# In all the above cases the default is to delete objects in a blocking way,
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# like if DEL was called. However you can configure each case specifically
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# in order to instead release memory in a non-blocking way like if UNLINK
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# was called, using the following configuration directives.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`lazyfree-lazy-eviction no
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`lazyfree-lazy-expire no
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`lazyfree-lazy-server-del no
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`replica-lazy-flush no
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# It is also possible, for the case when to replace the user code DEL calls
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# with UNLINK calls is not easy, to modify the default behavior of the DEL
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# command to act exactly like UNLINK, using the following configuration
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# directive:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`lazyfree-lazy-user-del no
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`################################ THREADED I/O #################################
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Redis is mostly single threaded, however there are certain threaded
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# operations such as UNLINK, slow I/O accesses and other things that are
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# performed on side threads.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Now it is also possible to handle Redis clients socket reads and writes
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# in different I/O threads. Since especially writing is so slow, normally
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Redis users use pipelining in order to speed up the Redis performances per
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# core, and spawn multiple instances in order to scale more. Using I/O
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# threads it is possible to easily speedup two times Redis without resorting
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# to pipelining nor sharding of the instance.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# By default threading is disabled, we suggest enabling it only in machines
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# that have at least 4 or more cores, leaving at least one spare core.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Using more than 8 threads is unlikely to help much. We also recommend using
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# threaded I/O only if you actually have performance problems, with Redis
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# instances being able to use a quite big percentage of CPU time, otherwise
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# there is no point in using this feature.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# So for instance if you have a four cores boxes, try to use 2 or 3 I/O
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# threads, if you have a 8 cores, try to use 6 threads. In order to
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# enable I/O threads use the following configuration directive:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# io-threads 4
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Setting io-threads to 1 will just use the main thread as usual.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# When I/O threads are enabled, we only use threads for writes, that is
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# to thread the write(2) syscall and transfer the client buffers to the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# socket. However it is also possible to enable threading of reads and
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# protocol parsing using the following configuration directive, by setting
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# it to yes:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# io-threads-do-reads no
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Usually threading reads doesn't help much.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# NOTE 1: This configuration directive cannot be changed at runtime via
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# CONFIG SET. Aso this feature currently does not work when SSL is
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# enabled.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# NOTE 2: If you want to test the Redis speedup using redis-benchmark, make
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# sure you also run the benchmark itself in threaded mode, using the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# --threads option to match the number of Redis threads, otherwise you'll not
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# be able to notice the improvements.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`############################ KERNEL OOM CONTROL ##############################
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# On Linux, it is possible to hint the kernel OOM killer on what processes
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# should be killed first when out of memory.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Enabling this feature makes Redis actively control the oom_score_adj value
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# for all its processes, depending on their role. The default scores will
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# attempt to have background child processes killed before all others, and
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# replicas killed before masters.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Redis supports three options:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# no:       Don't make changes to oom-score-adj (default).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# yes:      Alias to "relative" see below.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# absolute: Values in oom-score-adj-values are written as is to the kernel.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# relative: Values are used relative to the initial value of oom_score_adj when
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#           the server starts and are then clamped to a range of -1000 to 1000.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#           Because typically the initial value is 0, they will often match the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#           absolute values.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`oom-score-adj no
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# When oom-score-adj is used, this directive controls the specific values used
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# for master, replica and background child processes. Values range -2000 to
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 2000 (higher means more likely to be killed).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Unprivileged processes (not root, and without CAP_SYS_RESOURCE capabilities)
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# can freely increase their value, but not decrease it below its initial
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# settings. This means that setting oom-score-adj to "relative" and setting the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# oom-score-adj-values to positive values will always succeed.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`oom-score-adj-values 0 200 800
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`############################## APPEND ONLY MODE ###############################
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# By default Redis asynchronously dumps the dataset on disk. This mode is
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# good enough in many applications, but an issue with the Redis process or
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# a power outage may result into a few minutes of writes lost (depending on
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the configured save points).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The Append Only File is an alternative persistence mode that provides
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# much better durability. For instance using the default data fsync policy
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# (see later in the config file) Redis can lose just one second of writes in a
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# dramatic event like a server power outage, or a single write if something
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# wrong with the Redis process itself happens, but the operating system is
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# still running correctly.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# AOF and RDB persistence can be enabled at the same time without problems.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# If the AOF is enabled on startup Redis will load the AOF, that is the file
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# with the better durability guarantees.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Please check http://redis.io/topics/persistence for more information.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`appendonly no
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The name of the append only file (default: "appendonly.aof")
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`appendfilename "appendonly.aof"
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The fsync() call tells the Operating System to actually write data on disk
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# instead of waiting for more data in the output buffer. Some OS will really flush
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# data on disk, some other OS will just try to do it ASAP.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Redis supports three different modes:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# no: don't fsync, just let the OS flush the data when it wants. Faster.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# always: fsync after every write to the append only log. Slow, Safest.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# everysec: fsync only one time every second. Compromise.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The default is "everysec", as that's usually the right compromise between
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# speed and data safety. It's up to you to understand if you can relax this to
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# "no" that will let the operating system flush the output buffer when
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# it wants, for better performances (but if you can live with the idea of
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# some data loss consider the default persistence mode that's snapshotting),
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# or on the contrary, use "always" that's very slow but a bit safer than
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# everysec.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# More details please check the following article:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# http://antirez.com/post/redis-persistence-demystified.html
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# If unsure, use "everysec".
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# appendfsync always
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`appendfsync everysec
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# appendfsync no
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# When the AOF fsync policy is set to always or everysec, and a background
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# saving process (a background save or AOF log background rewriting) is
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# performing a lot of I/O against the disk, in some Linux configurations
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Redis may block too long on the fsync() call. Note that there is no fix for
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# this currently, as even performing fsync in a different thread will block
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# our synchronous write(2) call.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# In order to mitigate this problem it's possible to use the following option
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# that will prevent fsync() from being called in the main process while a
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# BGSAVE or BGREWRITEAOF is in progress.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# This means that while another child is saving, the durability of Redis is
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the same as "appendfsync none". In practical terms, this means that it is
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# possible to lose up to 30 seconds of log in the worst scenario (with the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# default Linux settings).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# If you have latency problems turn this to "yes". Otherwise leave it as
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# "no" that is the safest pick from the point of view of durability.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`no-appendfsync-on-rewrite no
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Automatic rewrite of the append only file.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Redis is able to automatically rewrite the log file implicitly calling
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# BGREWRITEAOF when the AOF log size grows by the specified percentage.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# This is how it works: Redis remembers the size of the AOF file after the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# latest rewrite (if no rewrite has happened since the restart, the size of
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the AOF at startup is used).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# This base size is compared to the current size. If the current size is
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# bigger than the specified percentage, the rewrite is triggered. Also
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# you need to specify a minimal size for the AOF file to be rewritten, this
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# is useful to avoid rewriting the AOF file even if the percentage increase
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# is reached but it is still pretty small.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Specify a percentage of zero in order to disable the automatic AOF
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# rewrite feature.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`auto-aof-rewrite-percentage 100
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`auto-aof-rewrite-min-size 64mb
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# An AOF file may be found to be truncated at the end during the Redis
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# startup process, when the AOF data gets loaded back into memory.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# This may happen when the system where Redis is running
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# crashes, especially when an ext4 filesystem is mounted without the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# data=ordered option (however this can't happen when Redis itself
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# crashes or aborts but the operating system still works correctly).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Redis can either exit with an error when this happens, or load as much
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# data as possible (the default now) and start if the AOF file is found
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# to be truncated at the end. The following option controls this behavior.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# If aof-load-truncated is set to yes, a truncated AOF file is loaded and
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the Redis server starts emitting a log to inform the user of the event.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Otherwise if the option is set to no, the server aborts with an error
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# and refuses to start. When the option is set to no, the user requires
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# to fix the AOF file using the "redis-check-aof" utility before to restart
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the server.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Note that if the AOF file will be found to be corrupted in the middle
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the server will still exit with an error. This option only applies when
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Redis will try to read more data from the AOF file but not enough bytes
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# will be found.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`aof-load-truncated yes
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# When rewriting the AOF file, Redis is able to use an RDB preamble in the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# AOF file for faster rewrites and recoveries. When this option is turned
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# on the rewritten AOF file is composed of two different stanzas:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   [RDB file][AOF tail]
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# When loading, Redis recognizes that the AOF file starts with the "REDIS"
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# string and loads the prefixed RDB file, then continues loading the AOF
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# tail.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`aof-use-rdb-preamble yes
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`################################ LUA SCRIPTING  ###############################
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Max execution time of a Lua script in milliseconds.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# If the maximum execution time is reached Redis will log that a script is
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# still in execution after the maximum allowed time and will start to
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# reply to queries with an error.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# When a long running script exceeds the maximum execution time only the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# SCRIPT KILL and SHUTDOWN NOSAVE commands are available. The first can be
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# used to stop a script that did not yet call any write commands. The second
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# is the only way to shut down the server in the case a write command was
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# already issued by the script but the user doesn't want to wait for the natural
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# termination of the script.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Set it to 0 or a negative value for unlimited execution without warnings.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`lua-time-limit 5000
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`################################ REDIS CLUSTER  ###############################
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Normal Redis instances can't be part of a Redis Cluster; only nodes that are
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# started as cluster nodes can. In order to start a Redis instance as a
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# cluster node enable the cluster support uncommenting the following:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# cluster-enabled yes
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Every cluster node has a cluster configuration file. This file is not
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# intended to be edited by hand. It is created and updated by Redis nodes.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Every Redis Cluster node requires a different cluster configuration file.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Make sure that instances running in the same system do not have
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# overlapping cluster configuration file names.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# cluster-config-file nodes-6379.conf
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Cluster node timeout is the amount of milliseconds a node must be unreachable
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# for it to be considered in failure state.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Most other internal time limits are a multiple of the node timeout.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# cluster-node-timeout 15000
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# A replica of a failing master will avoid to start a failover if its data
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# looks too old.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# There is no simple way for a replica to actually have an exact measure of
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# its "data age", so the following two checks are performed:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 1) If there are multiple replicas able to failover, they exchange messages
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    in order to try to give an advantage to the replica with the best
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    replication offset (more data from the master processed).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    Replicas will try to get their rank by offset, and apply to the start
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    of the failover a delay proportional to their rank.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 2) Every single replica computes the time of the last interaction with
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    its master. This can be the last ping or command received (if the master
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    is still in the "connected" state), or the time that elapsed since the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    disconnection with the master (if the replication link is currently down).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    If the last interaction is too old, the replica will not try to failover
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    at all.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The point "2" can be tuned by user. Specifically a replica will not perform
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the failover if, since the last interaction with the master, the time
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# elapsed is greater than:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   (node-timeout * cluster-replica-validity-factor) + repl-ping-replica-period
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# So for example if node-timeout is 30 seconds, and the cluster-replica-validity-factor
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# is 10, and assuming a default repl-ping-replica-period of 10 seconds, the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# replica will not try to failover if it was not able to talk with the master
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# for longer than 310 seconds.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# A large cluster-replica-validity-factor may allow replicas with too old data to failover
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# a master, while a too small value may prevent the cluster from being able to
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# elect a replica at all.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# For maximum availability, it is possible to set the cluster-replica-validity-factor
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# to a value of 0, which means, that replicas will always try to failover the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# master regardless of the last time they interacted with the master.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# (However they'll always try to apply a delay proportional to their
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# offset rank).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Zero is the only value able to guarantee that when all the partitions heal
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the cluster will always be able to continue.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# cluster-replica-validity-factor 10
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Cluster replicas are able to migrate to orphaned masters, that are masters
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# that are left without working replicas. This improves the cluster ability
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# to resist to failures as otherwise an orphaned master can't be failed over
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# in case of failure if it has no working replicas.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Replicas migrate to orphaned masters only if there are still at least a
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# given number of other working replicas for their old master. This number
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# is the "migration barrier". A migration barrier of 1 means that a replica
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# will migrate only if there is at least 1 other working replica for its master
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# and so forth. It usually reflects the number of replicas you want for every
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# master in your cluster.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Default is 1 (replicas migrate only if their masters remain with at least
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# one replica). To disable migration just set it to a very large value.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# A value of 0 can be set but is useful only for debugging and dangerous
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# in production.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# cluster-migration-barrier 1
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# By default Redis Cluster nodes stop accepting queries if they detect there
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# is at least a hash slot uncovered (no available node is serving it).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# This way if the cluster is partially down (for example a range of hash slots
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# are no longer covered) all the cluster becomes, eventually, unavailable.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# It automatically returns available as soon as all the slots are covered again.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# However sometimes you want the subset of the cluster which is working,
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# to continue to accept queries for the part of the key space that is still
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# covered. In order to do so, just set the cluster-require-full-coverage
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# option to no.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# cluster-require-full-coverage yes
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# This option, when set to yes, prevents replicas from trying to failover its
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# master during master failures. However the master can still perform a
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# manual failover, if forced to do so.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# This is useful in different scenarios, especially in the case of multiple
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# data center operations, where we want one side to never be promoted if not
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# in the case of a total DC failure.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# cluster-replica-no-failover no
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# This option, when set to yes, allows nodes to serve read traffic while the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the cluster is in a down state, as long as it believes it owns the slots. 
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# This is useful for two cases.  The first case is for when an application 
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# doesn't require consistency of data during node failures or network partitions.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# One example of this is a cache, where as long as the node has the data it
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# should be able to serve it. 
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The second use case is for configurations that don't meet the recommended  
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# three shards but want to enable cluster mode and scale later. A 
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# master outage in a 1 or 2 shard configuration causes a read/write outage to the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# entire cluster without this option set, with it set there is only a write outage.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Without a quorum of masters, slot ownership will not change automatically. 
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# cluster-allow-reads-when-down no
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# In order to setup your cluster make sure to read the documentation
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# available at http://redis.io web site.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`########################## CLUSTER DOCKER/NAT support  ########################
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# In certain deployments, Redis Cluster nodes address discovery fails, because
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# addresses are NAT-ted or because ports are forwarded (the typical case is
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Docker and other containers).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# In order to make Redis Cluster working in such environments, a static
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# configuration where each node knows its public address is needed. The
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# following two options are used for this scope, and are:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# * cluster-announce-ip
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# * cluster-announce-port
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# * cluster-announce-bus-port
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Each instructs the node about its address, client port, and cluster message
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# bus port. The information is then published in the header of the bus packets
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# so that other nodes will be able to correctly map the address of the node
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# publishing the information.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# If the above options are not used, the normal Redis Cluster auto-detection
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# will be used instead.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Note that when remapped, the bus port may not be at the fixed offset of
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# clients port + 10000, so you can specify any port and bus-port depending
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# on how they get remapped. If the bus-port is not set, a fixed offset of
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 10000 will be used as usual.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Example:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# cluster-announce-ip 10.1.1.5
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# cluster-announce-port 6379
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# cluster-announce-bus-port 6380
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`################################## SLOW LOG ###################################
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The Redis Slow Log is a system to log queries that exceeded a specified
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# execution time. The execution time does not include the I/O operations
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# like talking with the client, sending the reply and so forth,
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# but just the time needed to actually execute the command (this is the only
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# stage of command execution where the thread is blocked and can not serve
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# other requests in the meantime).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# You can configure the slow log with two parameters: one tells Redis
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# what is the execution time, in microseconds, to exceed in order for the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# command to get logged, and the other parameter is the length of the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# slow log. When a new command is logged the oldest one is removed from the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# queue of logged commands.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The following time is expressed in microseconds, so 1000000 is equivalent
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# to one second. Note that a negative number disables the slow log, while
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# a value of zero forces the logging of every command.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`slowlog-log-slower-than 10000
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# There is no limit to this length. Just be aware that it will consume memory.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# You can reclaim memory used by the slow log with SLOWLOG RESET.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`slowlog-max-len 128
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`################################ LATENCY MONITOR ##############################
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The Redis latency monitoring subsystem samples different operations
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# at runtime in order to collect data related to possible sources of
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# latency of a Redis instance.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Via the LATENCY command this information is available to the user that can
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# print graphs and obtain reports.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The system only logs operations that were performed in a time equal or
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# greater than the amount of milliseconds specified via the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# latency-monitor-threshold configuration directive. When its value is set
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# to zero, the latency monitor is turned off.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# By default latency monitoring is disabled since it is mostly not needed
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# if you don't have latency issues, and collecting data has a performance
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# impact, that while very small, can be measured under big load. Latency
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# monitoring can easily be enabled at runtime using the command
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# "CONFIG SET latency-monitor-threshold <milliseconds>" if needed.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`latency-monitor-threshold 0
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`############################# EVENT NOTIFICATION ##############################
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Redis can notify Pub/Sub clients about events happening in the key space.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# This feature is documented at http://redis.io/topics/notifications
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# For instance if keyspace events notification is enabled, and a client
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# performs a DEL operation on key "foo" stored in the Database 0, two
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# messages will be published via Pub/Sub:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# PUBLISH __keyspace@0__:foo del
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# PUBLISH __keyevent@0__:del foo
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# It is possible to select the events that Redis will notify among a set
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# of classes. Every class is identified by a single character:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  K     Keyspace events, published with __keyspace@<db>__ prefix.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  E     Keyevent events, published with __keyevent@<db>__ prefix.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  g     Generic commands (non-type specific) like DEL, EXPIRE, RENAME, ...
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  $     String commands
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  l     List commands
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  s     Set commands
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  h     Hash commands
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  z     Sorted set commands
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  x     Expired events (events generated every time a key expires)
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  e     Evicted events (events generated when a key is evicted for maxmemory)
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  t     Stream commands
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  m     Key-miss events (Note: It is not included in the 'A' class)
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  A     Alias for g$lshzxet, so that the "AKE" string means all the events
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#        (Except key-miss events which are excluded from 'A' due to their
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#         unique nature).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  The "notify-keyspace-events" takes as argument a string that is composed
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  of zero or multiple characters. The empty string means that notifications
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  are disabled.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  Example: to enable list and generic events, from the point of view of the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#           event name, use:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  notify-keyspace-events Elg
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  Example 2: to get the stream of the expired keys subscribing to channel
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#             name __keyevent@0__:expired use:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  notify-keyspace-events Ex
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  By default all notifications are disabled because most users don't need
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  this feature and the feature has some overhead. Note that if you don't
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#  specify at least one of K or E, no events will be delivered.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`notify-keyspace-events ""
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`############################### GOPHER SERVER #################################
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Redis contains an implementation of the Gopher protocol, as specified in
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the RFC 1436 (https://www.ietf.org/rfc/rfc1436.txt).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The Gopher protocol was very popular in the late '90s. It is an alternative
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# to the web, and the implementation both server and client side is so simple
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# that the Redis server has just 100 lines of code in order to implement this
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# support.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# What do you do with Gopher nowadays? Well Gopher never *really* died, and
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# lately there is a movement in order for the Gopher more hierarchical content
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# composed of just plain text documents to be resurrected. Some want a simpler
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# internet, others believe that the mainstream internet became too much
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# controlled, and it's cool to create an alternative space for people that
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# want a bit of fresh air.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Anyway for the 10nth birthday of the Redis, we gave it the Gopher protocol
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# as a gift.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# --- HOW IT WORKS? ---
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The Redis Gopher support uses the inline protocol of Redis, and specifically
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# two kind of inline requests that were anyway illegal: an empty request
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# or any request that starts with "/" (there are no Redis commands starting
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# with such a slash). Normal RESP2/RESP3 requests are completely out of the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# path of the Gopher protocol implementation and are served as usual as well.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# If you open a connection to Redis when Gopher is enabled and send it
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# a string like "/foo", if there is a key named "/foo" it is served via the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Gopher protocol.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# In order to create a real Gopher "hole" (the name of a Gopher site in Gopher
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# talking), you likely need a script like the following:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   https://github.com/antirez/gopher2redis
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# --- SECURITY WARNING ---
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# If you plan to put Redis on the internet in a publicly accessible address
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# to server Gopher pages MAKE SURE TO SET A PASSWORD to the instance.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Once a password is set:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   1. The Gopher server (when enabled, not by default) will still serve
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#      content via Gopher.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   2. However other commands cannot be called before the client will
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#      authenticate.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# So use the 'requirepass' option to protect your instance.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Note that Gopher is not currently supported when 'io-threads-do-reads'
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# is enabled.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# To enable Gopher support, uncomment the following line and set the option
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# from no (the default) to yes.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# gopher-enabled no
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`############################### ADVANCED CONFIG ###############################
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Hashes are encoded using a memory efficient data structure when they have a
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# small number of entries, and the biggest entry does not exceed a given
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# threshold. These thresholds can be configured using the following directives.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`hash-max-ziplist-entries 512
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`hash-max-ziplist-value 64
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Lists are also encoded in a special way to save a lot of space.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The number of entries allowed per internal list node can be specified
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# as a fixed maximum size or a maximum number of elements.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# For a fixed maximum size, use -5 through -1, meaning:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# -5: max size: 64 Kb  <-- not recommended for normal workloads
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# -4: max size: 32 Kb  <-- not recommended
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# -3: max size: 16 Kb  <-- probably not recommended
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# -2: max size: 8 Kb   <-- good
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# -1: max size: 4 Kb   <-- good
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Positive numbers mean store up to _exactly_ that number of elements
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# per list node.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The highest performing option is usually -2 (8 Kb size) or -1 (4 Kb size),
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# but if your use case is unique, adjust the settings as necessary.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`list-max-ziplist-size -2
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Lists may also be compressed.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Compress depth is the number of quicklist ziplist nodes from *each* side of
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the list to *exclude* from compression.  The head and tail of the list
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# are always uncompressed for fast push/pop operations.  Settings are:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 0: disable all list compression
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 1: depth 1 means "don't start compressing until after 1 node into the list,
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    going from either the head or tail"
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    So: [head]->node->node->...->node->[tail]
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    [head], [tail] will always be uncompressed; inner nodes will compress.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 2: [head]->[next]->node->node->...->node->[prev]->[tail]
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    2 here means: don't compress head or head->next or tail->prev or tail,
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    but compress all nodes between them.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 3: [head]->[next]->[next]->node->node->...->node->[prev]->[prev]->[tail]
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# etc.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`list-compress-depth 0
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Sets have a special encoding in just one case: when a set is composed
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# of just strings that happen to be integers in radix 10 in the range
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# of 64 bit signed integers.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The following configuration setting sets the limit in the size of the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# set in order to use this special memory saving encoding.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`set-max-intset-entries 512
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Similarly to hashes and lists, sorted sets are also specially encoded in
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# order to save a lot of space. This encoding is only used when the length and
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# elements of a sorted set are below the following limits:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`zset-max-ziplist-entries 128
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`zset-max-ziplist-value 64
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# HyperLogLog sparse representation bytes limit. The limit includes the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 16 bytes header. When an HyperLogLog using the sparse representation crosses
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# this limit, it is converted into the dense representation.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# A value greater than 16000 is totally useless, since at that point the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# dense representation is more memory efficient.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The suggested value is ~ 3000 in order to have the benefits of
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the space efficient encoding without slowing down too much PFADD,
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# which is O(N) with the sparse encoding. The value can be raised to
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# ~ 10000 when CPU is not a concern, but space is, and the data set is
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# composed of many HyperLogLogs with cardinality in the 0 - 15000 range.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`hll-sparse-max-bytes 3000
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Streams macro node max size / items. The stream data structure is a radix
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# tree of big nodes that encode multiple items inside. Using this configuration
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# it is possible to configure how big a single node can be in bytes, and the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# maximum number of items it may contain before switching to a new node when
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# appending new stream entries. If any of the following settings are set to
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# zero, the limit is ignored, so for instance it is possible to set just a
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# max entires limit by setting max-bytes to 0 and max-entries to the desired
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# value.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`stream-node-max-bytes 4096
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`stream-node-max-entries 100
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Active rehashing uses 1 millisecond every 100 milliseconds of CPU time in
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# order to help rehashing the main Redis hash table (the one mapping top-level
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# keys to values). The hash table implementation Redis uses (see dict.c)
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# performs a lazy rehashing: the more operation you run into a hash table
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# that is rehashing, the more rehashing "steps" are performed, so if the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# server is idle the rehashing is never complete and some more memory is used
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# by the hash table.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The default is to use this millisecond 10 times every second in order to
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# actively rehash the main dictionaries, freeing memory when possible.
