`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# If unsure:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# use "activerehashing no" if you have hard latency requirements and it is
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# not a good thing in your environment that Redis can reply from time to time
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# to queries with 2 milliseconds delay.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# use "activerehashing yes" if you don't have such hard requirements but
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# want to free memory asap when possible.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`activerehashing yes
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The client output buffer limits can be used to force disconnection of clients
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# that are not reading data from the server fast enough for some reason (a
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# common reason is that a Pub/Sub client can't consume messages as fast as the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# publisher can produce them).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The limit can be set differently for the three different classes of clients:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# normal -> normal clients including MONITOR clients
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# replica  -> replica clients
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# pubsub -> clients subscribed to at least one pubsub channel or pattern
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The syntax of every client-output-buffer-limit directive is the following:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# client-output-buffer-limit <class> <hard limit> <soft limit> <soft seconds>
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# A client is immediately disconnected once the hard limit is reached, or if
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the soft limit is reached and remains reached for the specified number of
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# seconds (continuously).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# So for instance if the hard limit is 32 megabytes and the soft limit is
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 16 megabytes / 10 seconds, the client will get disconnected immediately
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# if the size of the output buffers reach 32 megabytes, but will also get
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# disconnected if the client reaches 16 megabytes and continuously overcomes
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the limit for 10 seconds.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# By default normal clients are not limited because they don't receive data
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# without asking (in a push way), but just after a request, so only
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# asynchronous clients may create a scenario where data is requested faster
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# than it can read.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Instead there is a default limit for pubsub and replica clients, since
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# subscribers and replicas receive data in a push fashion.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Both the hard or the soft limit can be disabled by setting them to zero.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`client-output-buffer-limit normal 0 0 0
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`client-output-buffer-limit replica 256mb 64mb 60
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`client-output-buffer-limit pubsub 32mb 8mb 60
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Client query buffers accumulate new commands. They are limited to a fixed
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# amount by default in order to avoid that a protocol desynchronization (for
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# instance due to a bug in the client) will lead to unbound memory usage in
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the query buffer. However you can configure it here if you have very special
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# needs, such us huge multi/exec requests or alike.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# client-query-buffer-limit 1gb
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# In the Redis protocol, bulk requests, that are, elements representing single
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# strings, are normally limited to 512 mb. However you can change this limit
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# here, but must be 1mb or greater
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# proto-max-bulk-len 512mb
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Redis calls an internal function to perform many background tasks, like
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# closing connections of clients in timeout, purging expired keys that are
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# never requested, and so forth.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Not all tasks are performed with the same frequency, but Redis checks for
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# tasks to perform according to the specified "hz" value.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# By default "hz" is set to 10. Raising the value will use more CPU when
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Redis is idle, but at the same time will make Redis more responsive when
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# there are many keys expiring at the same time, and timeouts may be
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# handled with more precision.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The range is between 1 and 500, however a value over 100 is usually not
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# a good idea. Most users should use the default of 10 and raise this up to
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 100 only in environments where very low latency is required.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`hz 10
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Normally it is useful to have an HZ value which is proportional to the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# number of clients connected. This is useful in order, for instance, to
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# avoid too many clients are processed for each background task invocation
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# in order to avoid latency spikes.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Since the default HZ value by default is conservatively set to 10, Redis
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# offers, and enables by default, the ability to use an adaptive HZ value
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# which will temporarily raise when there are many connected clients.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# When dynamic HZ is enabled, the actual configured HZ will be used
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# as a baseline, but multiples of the configured HZ value will be actually
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# used as needed once more clients are connected. In this way an idle
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# instance will use very little CPU time while a busy instance will be
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# more responsive.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`dynamic-hz yes
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# When a child rewrites the AOF file, if the following option is enabled
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the file will be fsync-ed every 32 MB of data generated. This is useful
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# in order to commit the file to the disk more incrementally and avoid
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# big latency spikes.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`aof-rewrite-incremental-fsync yes
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# When redis saves RDB file, if the following option is enabled
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the file will be fsync-ed every 32 MB of data generated. This is useful
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# in order to commit the file to the disk more incrementally and avoid
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# big latency spikes.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`rdb-save-incremental-fsync yes
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Redis LFU eviction (see maxmemory setting) can be tuned. However it is a good
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# idea to start with the default settings and only change them after investigating
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# how to improve the performances and how the keys LFU change over time, which
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# is possible to inspect via the OBJECT FREQ command.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# There are two tunable parameters in the Redis LFU implementation: the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# counter logarithm factor and the counter decay time. It is important to
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# understand what the two parameters mean before changing them.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The LFU counter is just 8 bits per key, it's maximum value is 255, so Redis
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# uses a probabilistic increment with logarithmic behavior. Given the value
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# of the old counter, when a key is accessed, the counter is incremented in
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# this way:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 1. A random number R between 0 and 1 is extracted.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 2. A probability P is calculated as 1/(old_value*lfu_log_factor+1).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 3. The counter is incremented only if R < P.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The default lfu-log-factor is 10. This is a table of how the frequency
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# counter changes with a different number of accesses with different
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# logarithmic factors:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# +--------+------------+------------+------------+------------+------------+
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# | factor | 100 hits   | 1000 hits  | 100K hits  | 1M hits    | 10M hits   |
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# +--------+------------+------------+------------+------------+------------+
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# | 0      | 104        | 255        | 255        | 255        | 255        |
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# +--------+------------+------------+------------+------------+------------+
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# | 1      | 18         | 49         | 255        | 255        | 255        |
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# +--------+------------+------------+------------+------------+------------+
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# | 10     | 10         | 18         | 142        | 255        | 255        |
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# +--------+------------+------------+------------+------------+------------+
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# | 100    | 8          | 11         | 49         | 143        | 255        |
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# +--------+------------+------------+------------+------------+------------+
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# NOTE: The above table was obtained by running the following commands:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   redis-benchmark -n 1000000 incr foo
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#   redis-cli object freq foo
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# NOTE 2: The counter initial value is 5 in order to give new objects a chance
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# to accumulate hits.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The counter decay time is the time, in minutes, that must elapse in order
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# for the key counter to be divided by two (or decremented if it has a value
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# less <= 10).
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The default value for the lfu-decay-time is 1. A special value of 0 means to
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# decay the counter every time it happens to be scanned.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# lfu-log-factor 10
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# lfu-decay-time 1
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`########################### ACTIVE DEFRAGMENTATION #######################
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# What is active defragmentation?
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# -------------------------------
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Active (online) defragmentation allows a Redis server to compact the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# spaces left between small allocations and deallocations of data in memory,
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# thus allowing to reclaim back memory.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Fragmentation is a natural process that happens with every allocator (but
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# less so with Jemalloc, fortunately) and certain workloads. Normally a server
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# restart is needed in order to lower the fragmentation, or at least to flush
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# away all the data and create it again. However thanks to this feature
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# implemented by Oran Agra for Redis 4.0 this process can happen at runtime
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# in a "hot" way, while the server is running.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Basically when the fragmentation is over a certain level (see the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# configuration options below) Redis will start to create new copies of the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# values in contiguous memory regions by exploiting certain specific Jemalloc
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# features (in order to understand if an allocation is causing fragmentation
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# and to allocate it in a better place), and at the same time, will release the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# old copies of the data. This process, repeated incrementally for all the keys
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# will cause the fragmentation to drop back to normal values.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Important things to understand:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 1. This feature is disabled by default, and only works if you compiled Redis
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    to use the copy of Jemalloc we ship with the source code of Redis.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    This is the default with Linux builds.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 2. You never need to enable this feature if you don't have fragmentation
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    issues.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# 3. Once you experience fragmentation, you can enable this feature when
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#    needed with the command "CONFIG SET activedefrag yes".
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# The configuration parameters are able to fine tune the behavior of the
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# defragmentation process. If you are not sure about what they mean it is
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# a good idea to leave the defaults untouched.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Enabled active defragmentation
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# activedefrag no
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Minimum amount of fragmentation waste to start active defrag
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# active-defrag-ignore-bytes 100mb
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Minimum percentage of fragmentation to start active defrag
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# active-defrag-threshold-lower 10
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Maximum percentage of fragmentation at which we use maximum effort
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# active-defrag-threshold-upper 100
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Minimal effort for defrag in CPU percentage, to be used when the lower
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# threshold is reached
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# active-defrag-cycle-min 1
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Maximal effort for defrag in CPU percentage, to be used when the upper
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# threshold is reached
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# active-defrag-cycle-max 25
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Maximum number of set/hash/zset/list fields that will be processed from
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the main dictionary scan
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# active-defrag-max-scan-fields 1000
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Jemalloc background thread for purging will be enabled by default
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`jemalloc-bg-thread yes
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# It is possible to pin different threads and processes of Redis to specific
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# CPUs in your system, in order to maximize the performances of the server.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# This is useful both in order to pin different Redis threads in different
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# CPUs, but also in order to make sure that multiple Redis instances running
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# in the same host will be pinned to different CPUs.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Normally you can do this using the "taskset" command, however it is also
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# possible to this via Redis configuration directly, both in Linux and FreeBSD.
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# You can pin the server/IO threads, bio threads, aof rewrite child process, and
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the bgsave child process. The syntax to specify the cpu list is the same as
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# the taskset command:
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`#
`}),(0,n.jsx)(e.span,{className:"code-line",children:`# Set redis server/io threads to cpu affinity 0,2,4,6:
